# Formative Assessment for One-Room Schoolhouses

## Section 1 — What formative assessment is

Formative assessment is the ongoing, low-stakes feedback loop a teacher runs
during instruction to sense whether students are actually tracking with the
lesson. It is distinct from summative assessment (tests, final grades) in that
its purpose is correction and adjustment, not ranking.

## Section 2 — Why it matters in a multi-age classroom

A one-room schoolhouse teacher has to hold four-to-six distinct developmental
bands at once. Formative feedback is the primary lever that lets a single
teacher detect when one age group has lost the plot before the whole lesson
drifts. Without it, the teacher over-relies on the oldest students' nods.

## Section 3 — Techniques that carry little cognitive load

- Thumb-check (thumbs up / sideways / down after a question)
- Color-card (each student holds a red / yellow / green card at their desk)
- One-sentence exit ticket
- Pair-and-share with a structured listen-back

Each of these takes under two minutes and surfaces at least three signal
bands per check.

## Section 4 — Common mistakes

The most common mistake is calling on the same three confident students. A
classroom in which only those three voices speak is not a classroom the
teacher is reading — it is a classroom the teacher is being performed at.
The second most common mistake is confusing compliance (quiet, looking
forward) with understanding.

## Section 5 — A worked example: fractions across grade 3 and grade 5

In a combined 3-5 classroom introducing equivalent fractions, a thumb-check
after two worked examples surfaces the grade-3 block (most showing thumbs
sideways) while the grade-5 students are ready to advance. The teacher then
splits: grade-5 to independent practice, grade-3 to a guided manipulative
(paper strips folded in halves and quarters).

## Section 6 — How to close the feedback loop

Feedback is only formative if the teacher acts on it. The act can be as
small as re-phrasing one sentence, pulling one student aside for thirty
seconds, or moving a whole group to a different activity. The reflex is:
sense → decide → adjust, all in under five minutes.

## Section 7 — Habits to build

The habits that stick are the ones small enough to not need planning: end
every ten-minute block with a signal-check; open every new concept with
a retrieval prompt; close every day with one sentence the student writes
about what they noticed themselves doing. These three habits compound over
a school year.
